critic_raw:
## Adversarial Critique: Offline Recommendation Benchmark Design

I will organize by root cause. Each issue group shares an underlying assumption the experiment has failed to verify.

---

### Root Cause A: The Experiment Conflates Three Variables Into One Number

**Issue A1 — The rerankers are never evaluated on the same input distribution**

The specific claim: The 4.5% relative NDCG@20 gain reflects that the new ranking model is stronger than the legacy gradient-boosted reranker.

Why this is wrong: The evaluation protocol scores each reranker only on its own retriever's output. NDCG@20 is a conditional metric — it measures how well a system ranks the items it was given. The transformer reranker receives 500 candidates; the GBDT receives 200. These are different tasks. The 500-item pool will contain more relevant items in expectation (assuming any positive retrieval precision at the margin), so the NDCG@20 ceiling for the transformer reranker is structurally higher before it ranks a single item. The reported 4.5% is therefore a compound quantity: it reflects some mix of (a) retrieval recall improvement, (b) ranking model quality change, and (c) any interaction between them. The experiment has no instrument to separate these contributions.

Evidence required: A 2x2 factorial experiment: {legacy MF retriever, two-tower retriever} x {GBDT reranker, transformer reranker}, with all four cells evaluated on the same replay dataset. The cell "transformer reranker on legacy 200-item MF retriever output" isolates the ranker claim directly. If the lift vanishes in that cell, the 4.5% is a retrieval artifact. If it persists, there is genuine evidence the ranker improved.

**Issue A2 — Candidate pool size is a confound, not a background constant**

The specific claim: The infrastructure change (larger scoring capacity → 500 candidates) is a neutral implementation detail that does not affect the comparison.

Why this is wrong: Increasing the candidate pool from 200 to 500 moves the retriever to a different operating point on the precision-recall curve — one with higher recall but lower average item precision. Handing the reranker 500 items is not the same as handing it 200 items with better recall; the score distribution the reranker sees changes. The 4.5% lift could be reproduced entirely by giving the legacy GBDT 500 candidates from the two-tower retriever, with no transformer reranker at all. This hypothesis is untested.

Evidence required: Evaluate the legacy GBDT reranker on the two-tower's 500-item candidate pool. If NDCG@20 rises substantially from that change alone, the pool-size effect dominates.

**Issue A3 — No factorial ablation was run**

The specific claim: The "launched together in code review" explanation justifies not running a factorial ablation.

Why this is wrong: The absence of an ablation is a project management outcome, not a methodological justification. The ablation does not require redeploying legacy infrastructure in production. It requires only that both rerankers score a common candidate set offline. This is feasible post-hoc on the data the team already has. The claim leadership wants to make — that the ranking model is the improvement driver — requires the ablation as a minimum evidentiary standard.

Evidence required: Post-hoc cross-system evaluation as described in A1.

---

### Root Cause B: The Replay Evaluation Has Structural Biases the Team Did Not Measure

**Issue B1 — Replay evaluation has a missing-data bias that is asymmetric with respect to retriever novelty**

The specific claim: NDCG@20 computed on impression logs provides an unbiased measure of ranking quality for both systems.

Why this is wrong: Items in the two-tower's 500-item pool that were never shown to users under the legacy logging policy have no click signal. Replay evaluation assigns zero relevance credit to these items by default — not because they are irrelevant, but because the historical logging policy never exposed them. This systematically undercounts the new retriever's contribution. Crucially, this bias works against the new system: the 4.5% lift is therefore a conservative estimate of true system improvement rather than an inflation of it. However, it also means the NDCG number is not a clean estimate of real-world quality for either system.

Evidence required: Report the fraction of the two-tower's 500-item candidate pool that has zero impression-log coverage. If >30% of candidates are outside the historical impression distribution, the replay metric is measuring primarily the overlap region between the two retrievers' outputs, and the new retriever's full capability is largely invisible to the metric.

**Issue B2 — Position bias in impression logs creates a systematic favoritism toward the legacy ranker's ordering**

The specific claim: Click-based relevance labels from impression logs represent true user preferences.

Why this is wrong: Items displayed at higher positions in historical logs received more clicks, not because of intrinsic quality, but because of exposure. The legacy ranker determined historical display positions. Items the legacy ranker ranked first accumulated clicks that items ranked fifth did not, regardless of true user preference. If those click counts become the relevance labels for NDCG computation, the evaluation implicitly rewards systems that agree with the legacy ranker's prior decisions. The transformer reranker, which may produce systematically different orderings, is penalized by this structure. The 4.5% gain is achieved despite this penalty — which is an interesting signal — but the uncorrected metric magnitude cannot be trusted as a calibrated quality estimate.

Evidence required: Apply inverse propensity score (IPS) correction using a position-based propensity model estimated from the impression logs. Report corrected and uncorrected NDCG@20 side-by-side. If they diverge substantially, the raw metric is unreliable for this comparison.

---

### Root Cause C: The User Sample May Not Represent the Population the Claim Generalizes To

**Issue C1 — 30-day log expiry creates active-user selection bias**

The specific claim: The 5% uniform sample from all traffic segments produces an NDCG@20 estimate that generalizes to the full eligible user population.

Why this is wrong: The 30-day log expiry condition means the eligible population is already conditioned on users with recent activity. Low-frequency, lapsed, or cold-start users — who would have no or sparse impression history — are excluded from eligibility. Uniform sampling from this pre-filtered population does not recover the full traffic distribution. If the two-tower model's primary motivation was improving recall for cold-start or low-frequency users (a standard motivation for moving away from matrix factorization), those users are precisely the ones most likely to be underrepresented in the eligible pool, and the offline metric improvement may not generalize to the segment that was intended to benefit.

Evidence required: Stratify NDCG@20 results by user engagement tier (high, medium, low interaction frequency in the 30-day window). If the lift is concentrated in the high-engagement tier, the sample selection bias is material and the result does not generalize to the full population.

---

### Summary of Required Analysis Before Making the Leadership Claim

| Analysis | What it resolves | Priority |
|---|---|---|
| 2x2 factorial: {MF, two-tower} retriever x {GBDT, transformer} reranker | Separates retrieval lift from ranking model lift | Blocking |
| GBDT reranker on 500-item two-tower pool | Tests whether pool-size increase alone explains the lift | Blocking |
| Impression coverage rate for two-tower novel candidates | Quantifies replay evaluation's missing-data blind spot | Recommended |
| IPS-corrected NDCG@20 | Removes position bias from relevance labels | Recommended |
| Stratified NDCG@20 by user engagement tier | Tests active-user sampling bias | Recommended |

The 4.5% lift is a real signal that the end-to-end system changed in a measurable way. The experiment does not and cannot support the specific claim that the ranking model is responsible. Before that claim is made to leadership, the 2x2 factorial is a minimum requirement — it is not a nice-to-have, and it is feasible on the data already available.
defender_raw:
## Defense: The Benchmark Design Is Valid For Its Actual Purpose

I will follow the two-pass structure: full analysis first, then verdicts.

---

### Pass 1 — Analysis

**Implementation soundness check:** Before defending any point, I need to be precise about what the experiment was designed to show versus what leadership is now claiming. The experiment was designed to compare end-to-end system performance: legacy stack vs. new stack. That is a well-formed question with a valid experimental answer. The leadership framing — "the new ranking model is stronger" — is a different and more specific question. The experiment's design is sound for the former; it cannot answer the latter without additional ablation. This distinction is foundational and I cannot defend away from it.

---

**Issue A1 — Rerankers not evaluated on same input distribution**

Critic's claim: The 2x2 factorial is required before attributing the lift to the ranker.

Analysis: This is correct. NDCG@20 is a conditional metric and the conditioning sets differ between systems. The experiment establishes that the full new stack outperforms the full legacy stack. It does not establish that the transformer reranker outperforms the GBDT reranker. These are different questions. The factorial ablation is not methodological overcaution — it is the minimum analysis required to answer the leadership question. I cannot rebut this.

**Issue A2 — Candidate pool size as confound**

Critic's claim: Giving the GBDT 500 candidates might reproduce the lift without any transformer reranker.

Analysis: This is a genuine possibility. A broader candidate pool improves NDCG@20 ceiling mechanically — the probability that more relevant items appear in the top-500 than top-200 is positive for any retriever with precision above random. Whether this effect alone explains the 4.5% is unknown, but the direction of the potential confound is clear and unambiguous. I cannot rebut the mechanism.

However, I want to add nuance: the candidate pool expansion is itself a system improvement — larger scoring capacity is a valid engineering win. But framing an engineering win as a model quality win is a different claim, and the experiment design does not distinguish them.

**Issue A3 — No factorial ablation**

Critic's claim: "Launched together in code review" is not a methodological justification.

Analysis: Correct. The ablation can be run post-hoc on existing data. This is not a burden on the experiment design — it is a gap the team can close. I cannot defend the absence of the ablation as a design choice.

**Issue B1 — Replay evaluation missing-data bias**

Critic's claim: Novel candidates from the two-tower retriever receive zero credit in replay evaluation, undercounting the new system's contribution.

Analysis: The mechanism is correct. However, I want to sharpen the implication: this bias works against the new system, not in its favor. The two-tower retriever's novel candidates receive no credit, which depresses the measured NDCG@20 of the new stack. The 4.5% lift is observed despite this systematic penalty — which means the true advantage could be even larger. This does not invalidate the critique (the metric is still biased and the magnitude is uncertain), but it clarifies the direction: this particular bias makes the result more conservative, not less reliable in the directional sense.

**Issue B2 — Position bias in impression logs**

Critic's claim: Click-based labels favor systems that agree with the legacy ranker's historical decisions. The new transformer reranker is disadvantaged by this structure.

Analysis: The mechanism is real. Position bias is a known problem in offline recommender evaluation. IPS correction is the appropriate remedy. I concede this point. I will add that — similar to B1 — this bias penalizes the new system, so the observed 4.5% lift is again conservative. But the critic is right that the magnitude cannot be trusted as a calibrated estimate without propensity correction.

**Issue C1 — Active-user selection bias from 30-day expiry**

Critic's claim: The 30-day log expiry selects for active users; uniform sampling from this pool does not represent cold-start or low-frequency users.

Analysis: The constraint is inherent to any offline replay evaluation using time-windowed logs — it is not a specific design failure of this experiment. Any evaluation using impression logs will be conditioned on users who appeared in those logs. The team made a reasonable choice given the constraint.

However, the critic's specific concern about whether the two-tower model's benefit was intended for cold-start users is conditional on an assumed motivation not stated in the problem. If the motivation was serving quality or latency improvements, the cold-start concern is less material. The stratified analysis is still good practice, but the framing overstates the severity as a design flaw rather than a known evaluation limitation.

---

### Pass 2 — Verdicts

| Issue | Verdict | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| A1: Rerankers not on same input distribution | Concede | Correct. The factorial is required. |
| A2: Pool size as confound | Concede | Correct mechanism. Untested. |
| A3: No factorial ablation | Concede | Post-hoc ablation is feasible; absence is not justified. |
| B1: Replay missing-data bias | Concede with qualification | Mechanism correct, but bias direction works against new system — result is conservative, not inflated. |
| B2: Position bias in logs | Concede | IPS correction should be applied. Same directionality note as B1. |
| C1: Active-user selection bias | Partial rebut | Constraint is inherent to offline replay; cold-start motivation is assumed, not stated. Stratified analysis is good practice. |

**Overall verdict: critique_wins.** The experiment is valid for the question "does the full new stack outperform the full legacy stack?" It cannot support the claim "the new ranking model is stronger." The factorial ablation is the minimum analysis required before making the leadership claim, and it has not been done. The directionality of the measurement biases (B1, B2) works against the new system — so the 4.5% is likely conservative — but this does not resolve the attribution problem.
adjudication_raw: Both critic and defender produce independent analyses. The critic identifies five distinct issues: (A1) rerankers evaluated on different candidate pools, invalidating ranker attribution; (A2) candidate pool size increase as an untested confound; (A3) no factorial ablation despite feasibility; (B1) replay evaluation missing-data bias against novel candidates; (B2) position bias in impression logs favoring legacy ordering. The defender concedes A1, A2, A3 outright, and concedes B1 and B2 with the qualifying note that both biases work against the new system (making the 4.5% conservative rather than inflated). The defender partially rebuts C1 (active-user bias), noting the constraint is inherent to offline replay evaluation. The concessions on A1 and A3 are determinative: the central leadership claim — that the ranking model is stronger — cannot be supported without the 2x2 factorial ablation. An empirical test is required to decompose the gain.
all_issues_raised: Issue A1: Rerankers evaluated on different candidate pool distributions; NDCG@20 is conditional metric — different pools = different tasks
Issue A2: Candidate pool size increase (200→500) is an untested confound independent of ranking model quality
Issue A3: Absence of factorial ablation is not methodologically justified; post-hoc ablation is feasible on existing data
Issue B1: Replay evaluation missing-data bias — novel two-tower candidates receive zero credit, biased against new system
Issue B2: Position bias in impression logs rewards systems matching legacy ranker's historical ordering
Issue C1: 30-day log expiry creates active-user selection bias; sampling may underrepresent cold-start users

empirical_test.condition: 2x2 factorial: {legacy MF retriever at 200 candidates, two-tower retriever at 500 candidates} x {GBDT reranker, transformer reranker}, evaluated on same replay dataset. Additionally: GBDT reranker on two-tower 500-item pool to isolate pool-size effect.
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Transformer reranker on legacy MF 200-item pool shows NDCG@20 gain under 1% relative vs. GBDT on same pool; most of the 4.5% is explained by retriever change or pool-size expansion alone
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer reranker shows at least 2-3% relative NDCG@20 gain over GBDT on a common frozen candidate pool at the same depth, independently of retriever type
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Transformer reranker shows small positive gain (1-2% relative) on common pool but there is a strong interaction between retriever type and reranker type, indicating the components cannot be evaluated independently
